name: adhd-copilot-course-modules
source: ADHD Entrepreneurs — Hyperfocus V3 (Ali). Derived learning objects only; no raw transcript text retained here.

runtime_protocol.rule: Find the first incomplete module and recommend its next concrete checklist before generic planning.

phone: impulse apps removed, deleted, or buried behind friction
notifications near-zero except true urgent channels
environment: calendar and tasks are always visible on the default work surface
first work action can be started in one or two moves
clarity: goal lives in the trusted system
prioritization: next action attacks that bottleneck before lower-leverage work
time: each open loop is do_now, schedule, delegate, or park
scheduled items have a when and where
execution: active task has a first physical or digital step
misses are treated as system diagnostics
routines: morning and evening blocks are protected
each routine starts with an easy likable step
next_level: one repeated breakdown is selected for systematizing

triage_lanes.do_now: Fast and concrete enough to complete now.
triage_lanes.schedule: Important but not now; must receive a concrete time window and place.
triage_lanes.delegate: Better handled by another person, Codex, or automation; draft the ask.
triage_lanes.park: Not serving the current goal or not ripe; assign a review trigger.

spine.label: Subtract
spine.intent: Remove high-friction distraction before adding systems.

spine.label: Add
spine.intent: Build a trusted single system: goal, bottleneck, when/where, do-it.

spine.label: Multiply
spine.intent: Make it stick and compound with light structure.

modules.title: Phone Setup
modules.learning_objective: Re-engineer the phone so the first thing seen drives focus, not distraction.
concepts: You are driven by external cues; the first thing on your home screen dictates the next behavior.
Friction is a lever: make good defaults easy and impulse apps hard to reach.
skills: Make the home screen a single calendar widget.
Audit notifications app-by-app to near-zero (keep only true-urgent).
Remove/bury impulse apps behind pages of friction.
modules.misconception: A productivity app will fix focus. (Subtraction beats addition here.)
modules.apprentice_action: Draft a per-app notification audit checklist and a home-screen layout the user can apply in 10 minutes.
modules.transfer_context: Next time you unlock your phone, the calendar tells you what to do instead of a feed hijacking you.

modules.title: Environment Setup
modules.learning_objective: Arrange the physical space so doing the right thing is the path of least resistance.
concepts: Environment is the precursor to execution; cues in physical space determine behavior.
A persistent second surface (calendar + to-do always visible) offloads time-blindness and bad memory.
skills: Get a sturdy desk and comfortable chair (remove avoidance friction).
Keep calendar + to-do list always visible (second screen / monitor).
Place goal reminders and one-press triggers where you can't miss them.
modules.misconception: Motivation is internal. (It is mostly environmental cueing.)
modules.apprentice_action: Draft a one-page environment punch list (desk, always-on system, goal placement, one-press triggers).
modules.transfer_context: You stop relying on willpower because the room nudges the next action.

modules.title: Clarity
modules.learning_objective: Pick one trusted system and one in-your-face short-term goal.
concepts: Trust requires ONE all-encompassing system; scattered tools can't be trusted.
ADHD is a problem of WHEN and WHERE, not what and how — so tasks must live in a calendar.
Short-term goals beat distant ones for dopamine and momentum.
skills: Consolidate to a single system (e.g. calendar + tasks).
Write one short-term goal as an always-visible event.
If goal is unclear, invert: list what you do NOT want.
modules.misconception: Sophisticated tools mean more output. (Simplicity that you trust wins.)
modules.apprentice_action: Help the user phrase one short-term goal and where it will live so it's unmissable.
modules.transfer_context: You always know the one thing you're aiming at this quarter.

modules.title: Prioritization
modules.learning_objective: Always know what to work on next by naming the single bottleneck.
concepts: The fundamental algorithm: ask 'what is stopping me?' and attack only that.
Relentless focus on one bottleneck beats spreading effort thin.
skills: State the goal, then list what's missing.
Pick the one bottleneck that moves the needle.
Pour resources at that single bottleneck until it's gone.
modules.misconception: Do a bit of everything. (Sequence by bottleneck instead.)
modules.apprentice_action: Run the 'what is stopping me?' diagnosis on the user's goal and propose the single bottleneck to attack.
modules.transfer_context: Every morning you know the one needle-mover, never overwhelmed by the list.

modules.title: Time
modules.learning_objective: Apply the do-it-now-or-schedule-it (opt-out) rule to every task.
concepts: Opt-out, not opt-in: you must either do it now or schedule a when — never leave it floating.
Two lists: 'opt out' (incoming) and 'scheduled'. Scheduling closes the open loop and frees working memory.
skills: For each task ask: can I do it right now?
If not, assign a concrete time and move it to 'scheduled'.
Trust the system so you stop holding tasks in your head.
modules.misconception: I'll get to it later. ('Sometime' never comes; assign a when.)
modules.apprentice_action: Take the user's open loops and sort each into do-now or a proposed scheduled time block.
modules.transfer_context: Nothing rots on a list; every commitment has a home in time.

modules.title: Execution
modules.learning_objective: Start on demand using first-step framing, ownership, selling-yourself, and self-love.
concepts: Environment is the precursor; self-talk is the second half.
Think only of the FIRST step (a pebble, not a boulder) to summon enough dopamine.
Reframe 'have to' → 'get to / want to' (ownership over discipline).
Sell yourself on the immediate benefit; never beat yourself up — blame the system, stay curious.
skills: Shrink any task to its trivially-small first step.
Rephrase tasks as choices you want.
Attach the immediate benefit to the task.
On a missed day, diagnose the system instead of self-criticism.
modules.misconception: Discipline = forcing yourself. (Ownership + first step + self-love work better for ADHD.)
modules.apprentice_action: Rewrite the user's stuck task as a first-step + an ownership reframe + the immediate benefit.
modules.transfer_context: You can start the hard thing in seconds and recover from a miss without spiraling.

modules.title: Routines
modules.learning_objective: Set minimal morning/evening structure that is fun and stick-to-able.
concepts: The morning begins in the evening; the evening routine sets up the next day.
Right balance of structure and spontaneity; protect unscheduled blocks.
Start with an easy, likable first step; the goal is consistency, not perfection (4–5/7 is a win).
skills: Define a short morning and evening routine starting with something you enjoy.
Protect 'my time' blocks where nobody can schedule you.
Pick one needle-mover for tomorrow the night before.
modules.misconception: Routines must be rigid and complete. (Minimal + fun + protected beats perfect.)
modules.apprentice_action: Draft a minimal morning + evening routine (easy first step, protected window, one needle-mover) from the user's constraints.
modules.transfer_context: Most days start and end on rails you actually enjoy, so the system survives bad days.

modules.title: Next Level
modules.learning_objective: Reinforce completion and convert recurring breakdowns into reusable systems.
concepts: ADHD thrives on positive reinforcement; celebrate finishing to generate momentum.
Recurring friction is a signal to build a reusable system (or Codex skill).
skills: Mark setup as complete and acknowledge the win.
Name one recurring breakdown worth systematizing next.
modules.misconception: You're done after setup. (Compounding comes from turning breakdowns into systems.)
modules.apprentice_action: Summarize what's now in place, celebrate it, and propose one recurring breakdown to turn into a reusable skill.
modules.transfer_context: Each loop you close makes the next one cheaper — the system compounds.
